sources.name: Wikipedia — Mega Man X2
sources.notes: Crystal Chip (dash in air), Zero's dash mechanics, new Maverick bosses.

sources.name: TASVideos — SNES / Mega Man X2
sources.notes: Physics documentation. Page returned 404 at time of research — data from training knowledge.

notes: Mega Man X2 uses the same engine as Mega Man X (SNES, 1993). Core physics constants are inherited.
SAME ENGINE AS MMX: Walk 1.5 px/f, dash 3.5 px/f, jump 5.0 px/f, gravity 0.25 px/f² (assumed same constants).
KEY ADDITION: Crystal Chip upgrade — allows X to dash in the air (previously required Air Dash upgrade in MMX).
KEY ADDITION: Counter Hunter bosses — three mid-boss Mavericks that can steal Zero's parts.
KEY ADDITION: Zero sub-boss appears if all three Counter Hunters are defeated first.
Walk speed, dash, jump, gravity assumed same as MMX from same engine. VERIFY from ROM disassembly.

x_physics._note: Same engine as MMX — all base physics values inherited. Only additions documented separately.

walk_speed.source: Assumed same as MMX from identical engine. VERIFY.

dash_speed.source: Assumed same as MMX from identical engine. VERIFY.
dash_speed.notes: Dash is available from start (same as MMX after Dash Boots upgrade — in X2, dash is default).

gravity.source: Confirmed as Mega Man series constant. VERIFY.

wall_kick.description: Jump off a wall while sliding — X launches off in the opposite direction.
wall_kick.notes: Identical mechanic to MMX. Same timing window.

crystal_chip._note: The key addition in X2. Allows air dashing — one of the most traversal-relevant upgrades.
crystal_chip.description: Upgrade found in Zero Virus Boss area or first boss. Enables X to dash horizontally while airborne.
crystal_chip.effect: Pressing dash while airborne launches X horizontally at dash speed (~3.5 px/f) — cancels current jump arc.
notes: Air dash is the single most important traversal tool in the X series once unlocked.
Standard dash replaces horizontal momentum with dash momentum — not additive.
The air dash's arrival in X2 vs X1 (where it required finding the upgrade in a specific stage) changes routing entirely.
Air dash + wall kick creates complex aerial movement paths not possible with standard jump.

zero._note: Zero appears as an NPC/enemy in X2. His saber mechanics are notable.
zero.description: Zero uses a beam saber (melee) vs X's buster (ranged). If encountered as boss, fight uses saber attacks.
notes: Zero is not playable in X2 (becomes playable in X3 and later).
Counter Hunters collect Zero's parts. If all three are defeated, Zero's parts are rebuilt — he becomes the late-game boss.
Zero's saber attacks have short range but high damage — a different threat profile from standard Maverick bosses.

upgrades.crystal_chip: Air dash capability — enables dash while airborne
upgrades.arm_upgrade: Enhances X's buster (faster charge, bigger shots depending on version)
upgrades.leg_upgrade: Dash Boots (may be default in X2 — VERIFY)
upgrades.head_upgrade: Helmet upgrade (absorbs some damage from ceiling spikes or enhances charge — VERIFY)

gold_armor.description: Collecting ALL upgrades fills out the armor for a bonus power-up effect.
gold_armor.notes: Classic Mega Man X bonus for completionist play.

special_weapons._note: 8 Mavericks = 8 special weapons, each with a rock-paper-scissors weakness system.
notes: Each Maverick weapon exploits the weakness of another boss.
MMX2 Mavericks: Overdrive Ostrich, Morph Moth, Magna Centipede, Wheel Gator, Bubble Crab, Flame Stag, Wire Sponge, Crystal Snail.
The weakness chain creates optimal boss ordering for challenge/no-damage runs.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: MMX2 demonstrates 'engine inheritance' — using an identical physics foundation but adding one significant mechanic (air dash) changes the entire traversal vocabulary. The game feels fresh despite being built on familiar bones.

The Crystal Chip / air dash shifts the skill ceiling: players who haven't internalized air dash still complete the game (it's findable early), but players who master it execute routes impossible without it. Optional power that separates skill levels.

Counter Hunter design — three mid-bosses that reward finding them before the endgame — creates a meta-objective layer on top of the standard 8-boss structure. 'Find the three hidden bosses first for the better ending' is a secondary win condition.

Zero's saber vs X's buster creates a meaningful stylistic contrast that foreshadows his eventual playability. Different tool, different threat profile, different feel — established in X2 even though Zero isn't playable.
